content.subtitle: Uses
items: The future (for fixed events and schedules)

content.subtitle: Examples
items: Water **boils** at one hundred degrees Celsius at sea level.
She **cycles** to work three times a week to stay fit.
He **manages** the accounts department at a firm in the city centre.
I know this seems strange, but I **do appreciate** everything you have done for us.
The conference **begins** at nine o'clock sharp tomorrow morning.
Call me as soon as you **get** to the office.
Johnson **passes** to Williams — and Williams **drives** it straight into the goal!
SCIENTISTS **DISCOVER** POSSIBLE LINK BETWEEN SLEEP AND MEMORY LOSS
You **press** the button once and **hold** it for three seconds to restart the device.
A woman **walks** into a shop and **asks** for something to help her sleep ...

content.subtitle: Emphatic Present Simple
content.description: Do/does is added before the bare infinitive to give extra force to a statement, usually to counter a claim or to show strong conviction.
examples: I know you think I don't care, but I **do care** about the outcome of this project.
She might seem calm, but she **does worry** about meeting her deadlines.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Actions happening at the moment of speaking
Temporary situations and actions happening around now
Annoying or surprising habits (usually with always)

content.subtitle: Examples
items: The team **is holding** its monthly briefing in the main conference room right now.
He **is renting** a small apartment near the station while he looks for something permanent.
Fewer and fewer young people **are buying** newspapers — most get their news online.
My brother **is always leaving** the kitchen in a mess after cooking dinner!
He walks into the office. Two colleagues **are arguing** quietly by the window ...
**Are you flying** to the conference in Madrid next month?
We'll begin the tour as soon as everyone **is standing** by the main entrance.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Situations and states that started in the past and are still true now
A series of actions continuing up to now
Completed actions at an unspecified time in the past
Completed actions where the result matters now

content.subtitle: Examples
items: She**'s lived** in the same neighbourhood for nearly twenty years.
The chef**'s prepared** three courses and plated two desserts so far this evening.
**Have** you ever **climbed** a mountain higher than two thousand metres?
The technician**'s repaired** the boiler, so we finally have hot water again.
The lab**'s just published** its findings in an international science journal.
Send me the details once you**'ve confirmed** the booking.

content.subtitle: Note on American English
content.description: American English commonly uses the simple past in situations where British English would prefer the present perfect.
examples: US: **Did** you **complete** the assignment yet? UK: **Have** you **completed** the assignment yet?
US: She already **called**. UK: She**'s** already **called**.

examples.note: American English often uses gotten as the past participle of get when expressing the meaning 'obtain', 'become' or 'move'.

examples: US: I **haven't gotten** your message yet. UK: I **haven't got** your message yet.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Actions and situations that started in the past and are still continuing (or have just stopped)
Emphasising how long an action has been going on

content.subtitle: Examples
items: He**'s been cycling** through the mountains since dawn without a proper break.
They**'ve been filming** the documentary for the past six months.
She won't feel confident on stage until she**'s been rehearsing** for a few more days.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: Use the present perfect simple to highlight how many times something has happened or what has been achieved, and the present perfect continuous to stress how long an activity has been taking place.

examples.correct: She**'s sent** twelve emails this morning. (number of completed actions)
She**'s been sending** emails all morning. (emphasising duration)

sections.title: Stative and Non-Stative Uses of Verbs

content.subtitle: Stative Verbs (generally used in simple tenses)
Watch out!
content.description: Certain verbs that are normally stative can be used as dynamic verbs when describing an action or process rather than a state. Examples: be, consider, feel, have, look, see, smell, taste, think, weigh.

examples.correct: The museum **has** an impressive collection of ancient artefacts. (state: possession)
The director **is having** a difficult conversation with the board right now. (action)
The soup **tastes** too salty — I think we need to add more water. (state: perception)
The chef **is tasting** the sauce to check if it needs more seasoning. (action)

sections.title: A: Present simple or continuous?
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: The sky looks very dark now. I think it **rains / is raining** somewhere along the coast.
The museum **opens / is opening** at ten o'clock on weekdays and at noon on Sundays.
We **don't usually hold / aren't usually holding** team briefings on public holidays.
My flatmate **always borrows / is always borrowing** my charger without ever asking permission!
Fewer and fewer young people **choose / are choosing** to buy their own homes these days.
Could I call you back in ten minutes? I **have / am having** dinner with my family right now.
The annual marathon **takes / is taking** place on the last Sunday of April every year.
Where **do you go / are you going** after work tonight? Have you made any plans?
Scientists **warn / are warning** that global temperatures **continue / are continuing** to climb at a worrying pace.
The local bookshop near the park **does / is doing** surprisingly well this quarter.

sections.title: B: Error correction
sections.instructions: Each sentence contains a grammar error in the bold section. Write the correct form.

items.sentence: My colleague **lose always** his keys before the morning shift — it drives everyone mad.
He **is never drinking** alcohol at parties — he always sticks to sparkling water.
The delivery van **is arriving** outside our building at nine o'clock every morning without fail.
The technicians **already finish** the maintenance check on all three machines.
I'm afraid the receptionist **is not knowing** the exact time of your appointment.
How long **are you working** in the research department? You seem very experienced.
She **is studying** law at university — it's a five-year degree programme.
The family **are owning** a large farm outside the city.

sections.title: C: Present perfect simple & continuous
sections.instructions: Complete the sentences using the correct form of the verb in brackets. Use the present perfect simple or present perfect continuous.

items.sentence: This is the first time (I / ever / visit) ...... a theme park of this size.
(you / ever / work) ...... as a volunteer at a music festival?
The dancers (prepare) ...... for the show all week — they really deserve a rest.
How many instruments (she / learn) ...... over the years? I know she's always been musical.
items.answer: has she learned / has she learnt

items.sentence: I (wait) ...... for this diagnosis for over a month — I'm relieved it's finally arrived.
We (not / decide) ...... on a venue for the conference yet.
(the engineers / test) ...... the new software all day, but there are still a number of bugs.
That's the third time this week (he / forget) ...... to lock the front door.
I can't believe (you / never / try) ...... sushi before! You're really missing out.
The average temperature in the region (drop) ...... steadily over the past few winters.

sections.title: D: Adverbs of time
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap to complete the sentences.

items.sentence: Have you ...... eaten a meal that was cooked entirely over an open fire?
I've ...... finished the report — I'll send it to you in a moment.
She's been working at the same hospital ...... she qualified as a nurse.
We've been stuck in this traffic jam ...... nearly an hour and have barely moved.
Have you booked your accommodation ......? There are only a few rooms left.
He's ...... waiting for a reply from the editor — it's been three weeks now.
So far this year, the team has ...... lost a single home game.
This is the first time I've ...... performed in front of such a large audience.
She's ...... read five chapters today and plans to finish the whole book tonight.
Have you visited any interesting exhibitions ......? I'm looking for recommendations.

sections.title: E: Multiple choice
sections.instructions: Choose the correct answer: A, B, C or D.

questions.sentence: She (1) ...... on an urgent assignment this week, so she won't be available until Monday.
Water (2) ...... at a lower temperature at high altitudes than it does at sea level.
The charity (3) ...... over fifty thousand pounds already this year.
It must be a fault — the alarm (4) ...... all night, which explains why we're exhausted.
The pharmacy on the corner (5) ...... at seven o'clock, so you should head over soon.
I'm worried — the wind (6) ...... stronger. The forecast mentioned a possible storm.
I (7) ...... the terms and conditions twice and I still have a few questions.
(8) ...... you ever ...... a speech in front of a large group of people?
My brother (9) ...... tennis every weekend — it's been part of his routine for years.
The laboratory (10) ...... new samples all week and the results are nearly ready.

sections.title: F: Stative verbs (cloze)
sections.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct form of a stative verb from the box. Use each verb once.

items.sentence: Everybody (1) ...... that regular exercise is essential for maintaining good health.
That bicycle (2) ...... to my neighbour — you should put it back where you found it.
I (3) ...... whether the delivery will arrive on time given the current strikes.
The fresh coffee (4) ...... amazing — did you use a different brand this morning?
This energy drink (5) ...... more caffeine than three standard cups of coffee.
How much (6) ...... a return flight to Barcelona ...... in the month of August?
Most students (7) ...... to receive written feedback rather than verbal comments.

sections.title: G: Matching (Stative verbs)
sections.instructions: Match each sentence beginning (1–8) with the correct ending (A–H).

items.sentence: We think **B** that this new policy will benefit everyone involved.
items.answer: B – that this new policy will benefit everyone involved.

items.sentence: The young athlete resembles **H** his grandfather — same build, same walk.
items.answer: H – his grandfather — same build, same walk.

items.sentence: That dictionary belongs **F** to the school library, not to me.
items.answer: F – to the school library, not to me.

items.sentence: The emergency kit contains **G** three sets of batteries and a charger.
items.answer: G – three sets of batteries and a charger.

items.sentence: I now weigh **A** slightly less than I did before I started the diet.
items.answer: A – slightly less than I did before I started the diet.

items.sentence: The garden smells **D** wonderful in the early morning.
items.answer: D – wonderful in the early morning.

items.sentence: This project matters **E** a great deal to the local residents.
items.answer: E – a great deal to the local residents.

items.sentence: I've been considering **C** whether to change careers at my age.
items.answer: C – whether to change careers at my age.

sections.title: H: Error correction (extra words)
sections.instructions: Each sentence contains one extra word that should not be there. Click the extra word.

items.sentence: She has been to training at the gym every single day this week.
She has been already submitted her research paper to the international journal.
He has just been returned from a two-week fieldwork trip in the mountains.
Have you ever have considered applying for a promotion at your current company?
This is the very first time our team has ever reached the national finals.
He is been reading the same chapter for an hour and still doesn't understand it.
The team has already distributed it the new schedule to all department heads.
We still haven't yet agreed on a name for the new product line.
They have been waiting for a response since from the day they sent the application.
We are living in this neighbourhood ever since we sold our house last spring.

sections.title: I: Cloze
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap to complete the text.

items.sentence: Sports technology (1) ...... transformed the way athletes train and compete in recent years.
Teams around the world (2) ...... been using performance-tracking software to analyse player data since the early 2000s.
The volume of data collected during a single match (3) ...... grown dramatically over the past decade.
Coaches (4) ...... found that wearable devices provide valuable insights into player fitness and recovery.
The governing body (5) ...... just approved new guidelines on the use of technology during competitions.
Research (6) ...... showing that athlete recovery times are improving thanks to these innovations.
How long (7) ...... data science been influencing decisions in professional sport?
So far, studies (8) ...... already demonstrated that technology gives teams a measurable competitive advantage.

sections.title: J: Key word transformation
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first, using the word given. Do not change the word given. Use between two and five words.

items.sentence:
This is the first time I have attended a professional boxing match.
**NEVER**
I ...... a professional boxing match before.

He started his Spanish lessons eight months ago and is still attending them.
**FOR**
He ...... eight months.

The last time she went to the market was three weeks ago.
**BEEN**
She ...... three weeks.
items.answer: hasn't been to the market for

items.sentence:
They moved into that house several years ago and are still living there.
**SINCE**
They ...... they bought it.

It bothers me that my sister keeps forgetting to return my things.
**ALWAYS**
My sister ...... my things.

The morning coach to the capital sets off at seven thirty.
**DEPARTS**
The morning coach to the capital ...... at seven thirty.

This is the most challenging trail we have ever walked on.
**EVER**
This is the most challenging trail we ...... on.
